sentences.text: Well, I got there the next day.
So they say, OK, lay down on the table.
And I laid down on the table.
And I thought they might give me something, or anything like that.
And they tied me down with one hand-- I think they tie my feet too.
But the one hand that I didn't-- you know, that they had to operate, they let-- held it loose.
And they went ahead with the knife and just cut it.
No nothing.
And I just like, jumped up.
My body, my upper portion, just jumped up.
And then they give me a sedative.
They smacked me right on the head and knocked me out.
And that was my sedative.
Yeah.
Oh, a couple minutes or something like that, till they finished it.
I don't know.
But I don't think they did any dressing.
They just drained it and things like that.
And they say, OK, you can get off.
And I got off the table.
I was laying on the table.
I got off of the table.
I was trying to put my shirt on, and I was just like-- I don't know how I managed to get dressed.
Whatever, I got dressed.
And then it was the next-- then was another thing that day.
I had to hide out because I was in the barracks.
I wasn't supposed to be in the barracks that day.
So for that day, I had to either do it here, there, do something to-- not to be suspicious to last for the day.
I just-- I just, you know, just did-- got back to the barracks, and if I see somebody come in-- well, the-- from-- the barrack, the guy from the barrack, the commander, they knew me already.
But if the SS would happen to go by, I, I had to do some work, like I'm working in the barrack that day.
What am I doing there?
Things like that.
So it was a long day.
In Birkenau.
Yeah, yeah.
Well, I knew them while I was there.
I, I don't think I would recognize any now.
You know, I really don't, you know?
But I knew them there because a lot of times, you know, while I was-- they knew the, the company that I was.
And they knew that my-- the kapo, [INAUDIBLE], they knew him.
They knew I was working.
So they knew he, he was doing some black market and things like that.
I don't know.
He was getting some money from the Sonderkommando or something.
The Sonderkommando, I don't know if you know what that is.
Tell me what it is, Sonderkommando.

A Sonderkommando is the people that when the people came to the gas chamber, they-- after the people were gassed, and then they were-- they were, even before, they were the people that guide them into the crematorium to everything.
And then they worked on the clothes.
They sorted the clothes, emptied the clothes, and everything.
And then I remember an incidence.
In fact, this incidence was a kid, was, I think, was my same age as I, maybe a year older, from my home town.
And he happened to be in the Sonderkommando.
And he was caught with stealing something.
And they put lighter fluid on him and burned him alive.
Just to make were sure that nobody steals.
Yes.
Well, I don't remember.
I think I heard about it, and I know of it from-- because we were just-- the place that I worked later on in the different-- was right in the back of the crematorium, like, like, next door.
The house next door was the crematorium.
And their-- the place where we do the work was right here, just like, maybe couple hundred feet, something like that.
Well, the things that-- the thing that was going through our mind, we-- nobody believed that we'd ever be here talking to you, that we'd ever be alive.
Because we didn't think that-- there's no-- there's no way they can leave-- let us survive and to be able to tell these stories.
So what we were hoping is when our time comes, maybe we'll be strong enough maybe to put up a little fight.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
That's-- that basically was the thing, you know?
In fact, by putting up a little fight, there, there was an uprising from the Sonderkommando in, in the, in the camp, you know?
There-- I don't know [INAUDIBLE].
And this was right in the back in that-- in that-- from that-- in that oven, the crematorium was right next to us.
And there was an uprising.
But it didn't-- it didn't last very long.
But-- and they-- some people run out and start running.
And the SS will-- they were-- mostly all were killed.
In fact, I was in the way when they were running out.
And this SS was-- he saw me.
He was going to shoot me too.
And then he realized that I'm not from there, from another.
So he ran after other ones and let me-- and I was alive.
So, I don't know.
It's by strange coincidence, you know?
Did you know anyone from the Sonderkommando--
Yeah.
Yeah, we know some people, you know, because we could even talk to them, because through the barb wire we could talk to them and things like that.
And you know, no matter how much they tell you, you're not supposed to do anything.
But still, once in a while they used to send, throw something over to us.
We had some diamonds or something like this, you know?
And that's how you managed to-- you know, anybody that survived didn't live from their rations.
Somehow, managed to get something a little extra, you know ?
Where did you keep it?
Where did you keep-- if you were able to get--
I don't know.
I was-- I was searched so many times.
I don't know.
I think I was searched maybe once a month, something.
Because they always thought I had something.
But for somehow, I, I don't remember whether I didn't have it, or I was hiding, or something, or they didn't-- they didn't find.
Oh, yeah.
Yeah, yeah.
We-- oh, yeah.
Definitely.
We-- I had two very close-- we met-- become very close friends-- that we were just kind of sticking together.
You know--
It's, it's a shame.
I should-- I should, but--
Oh, yeah.
One was Chaim.
And the other one I don't remember.
I know one was Chaim, you know?
I guess it was small talk.
I mean, you know, we were basically in the same-- in the same field, the same-- you know, oh-- [CLEARS THROAT] That [INAUDIBLE], you know, in that thing that I worked, he tried to escape.
And he did escape.
But when they were out of camp, him and another-- and a Polish guy-- and what happened, when they were-- they were away from Auschwitz.
They escaped, and they got away.
But when they were on the outside, they were on a farm.
The contact was supposed to have something like that.
For some reason, they thought they were-- whatever the reason, they thought they were German spies or something like that.
There were supposed to meet with partisans or something like that.
And they didn't.
And they got the-- they notified some of the Germans.
They were brought-- brought them back.
And being here, somebody else would have gotten killed.
But being it was him, a political prisoner, so they had him stand in front of the-- you know, when you were coming back to camp, you know, from the outside, they had him stand with a sign.
Here [? mama, ?] I'm back.
I'm back, you know?
Like [INAUDIBLE] I'm back.
And then they send them to a different-- and he wasn't charged.
So we were-- I wound up in a different outfit, you know, doing something else.
And that's how I met these other two guys, and we would become good friends, you know?
We stuck together, you know?
Tell me a little bit more about the work they had you do.
The first one was, again?
The first one was, like, digging, digging the ditches for, for, later, water pipes.
And the other one we were just, just pushing a wagon.
I don't know what we were doing.
Hauling stuff from one side to another side, you know?
I don't even remember.
Pipes or things like that, you know?
Well, they were building-- you know-- OK.
When I came into the camp, that was the old camp.
And then they built a new camp to hold more people.
Yes.
Still Birkenau, yeah.
To hold more people to bring more people in.
Because they kept bringing more people constantly, so they needed more room.
And they even had a women's camp there too.
It was just like a barb wire that separated you.
Yeah.
We saw and we talked to them.
In fact, one thing I'll never forget, that an incident happened to me.
The three of us, you know, that were close friends, Chaim, myself, and this other guy.
OK.
I was the youngest, Chaim was the oldest.
He was-- let's see.
If I was 15, 16, he might have been about 26 or 28.
The other guy may have been couple years older than I am.
And he used to talk to this one girl.
And once in a while, he used to throw her over some food, you know?
If we had something else, he would throw her something.
And naturally if you got caught, you got shot.
But that's one of those things.
And one of this, this other guy said to me, let's make a joke and throw her something over that's an empty box or something like that.
And I did it.
And I never forget this incidence.
And I will never forget that.
That from that day on, whatever I do something, I will always think twice.
Because he was upset at us.
He knew I didn't do it maliciously, but I think a kid-- but it wasn't a proper joke to do at that time.
You know, if you throw something over to that girl, some food, something to, to keep her alive, and she get caught, at least it's to stay alive.
But to throw something over or something like a joke, was in very poor taste.
I felt like, like getting in the ground and don't get up.
But I felt at that time.
No.
No, I remember she was a pretty girl and things like that, but no.
She laughed and everything like that about it.
But I don't know-- I don't know how he found out.
I think we must have told him, or she told him, something like that.
And he was very upset, and he had the right to be upset But when you're young, you do stupid things a lot of times.
Yeah.
Well, the barracks-- no.
No.
The crematorium, that's when we were outside the barracks.
Outside the barracks.
In the barracks, it was in--
Well--
In, in the barracks, the-- you know, one day when I was still with [INAUDIBLE] friends, you know, when we were working, at digging the ditches, and there was Polish people.
There were foreman's and like, engineers, and things like that that were in charge of the work.
And they were transporting a couple trucks of the sick people.
You know, like, when I was in the sanitarium, that time like this, and-- to the gas chambers, because they were-- I don't know how sick.
But they-- and they were-- they knew where they were going.
It was different when the people come from the outside.
They were were being--
They didn't know.
No matter how much you do, you didn't know.
But the people that were in camp, which these people were in camp.
And they were on open trucks.
And there was-- in that-- going-- it was this road that was right by where we were digging the ditches, by the road.
And they were screaming, you know, like these screams.
And I remembered this Pollack, the engineer, and he said, said to us, he says, there is no God.
If there's a God-- and he was a Pole, so pretty Catholic religious.
He says, if there's a God and he can't hear these screams, that something must be-- that there is no God.
I don't know what I was thinking.
I was just like-- I don't think I was thinking.
I mean, you see, you see these, I don't know, one, two, three, or four trucks with these half-starved, half-dead people, and-- but strong enough, enough strength to scream.
And they know where they're going.
Couldn't do nothing, couldn't do any fight, or anything like that because they were half-dead.
But you have done enough.
And I, I I don't remember what I was doing in there.
I wasn't the only one.
There was quite a few of us.
Yeah.
Oh, the screams stayed with you for a long time.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And, and, and the SS stayed you-- with you for a long time.
Not anyone in particular.
I mean, they were all-- you know, you see them with these dogs.
And you know, the dog is just-- he look at you and just-- It was-- and, and you get up in the morning, in the freeze, in the cold.
And, and you didn't have any clothes and any shoes, wooden shoes, and everything, you know?
It's-- [CLEARS THROAT] And they had-- they had assemblies constantly, you know, because they're picking up prisoners from here to send to this outlying camps to, to work and things like that.
I was-- in that situation I was fortunate.
[CLEARS THROAT] Because being-- I was working with this [INAUDIBLE] and the thing.
So a lot of time when they had assemblies to go out, the barrack-- the in charge of the barracks, he already-- he told them.
He says, leave him alone.
He doesn't have to go.
He says, he's German.
He doesn't have to go.
So I was able to not to go in the assemblies and was able to stay there for--
[LAUGHS] It wasn't that great, but at least I, I was alive.
He didn't-- yeah, I-- I think he was-- didn't, didn't wear the clothes that we had.
It was more of a different-- they had-- you know, like, we all had the, the signs like the stars.
And some of them was yellow, some of them was this.
And, and they, they had a badge, also a piece, like, a white pin.
And there's were even a triangle down.
Red was political.
Green was murderers.
You know, they were murderers and everything like that, but they were in charge of us, you know?
So it didn't [INAUDIBLE].
Like, if they had German people, like we have people now in jail, if they have murderers and things like that, they brought them over to this camp and made them in charge of this.
And they were given decent food and decent clothing and everything.
The stripes, the, the regular stripes.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Star of David, regular stripes.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Well, I actually had to wear it even in town before you were liquidated.
You had to wear the star.
In hometown, you had to wear the star too before you were liquidated.
And in ghetto, you had to wear it too.
Yes.
Yeah.
Till from March 14 till October '44.
And then when they started-- the Russians starting coming closer to Poland and things like that, I was-- it was by freight cars again-- we were liquidated.
And we were sent to-- I think it was a camp, for just a holding camp.
I think it was Sachsenhausen in Germany.
Yeah.
It, it was several days.
Like several days, like a train ride maybe, three, four days train ride, you know, to there, to Sachsenhausen.
Yeah.
But I, I wanted to-- in terms of Auschwitz still--
Yeah.
No, this was constantly.
It was all day long, you know?
It was-- the beatings and the things, you know?
I mean--
It was a bunk, you know?
It was about four, five in a-- but it was two bunks.
It was a one bunk and one underneath.
And it was about four or five people upstairs, and four or five people downstairs.
It had a blanket.
I think they used to give you clothes once a week or something.
Take a shower and a cold-- in the barracks were just like-- there was no bathrooms or anything, you know?
It's just-- What was pervasive in terms of smell there?
Was it--
That's-- it was-- I, I don't remember.
Mm.
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
Then--
And then we left and we--
Auschwitz by train.
I think we-- still these two guys.
You know, we were still with us, because when we got to this other camp, you know, we got to the other camp, and we were-- we kind of stuck together.
And when we got to the other camp-- you know, I think it took about three days.
But we went to Sachsenhausen.
But in Sachsenhausen, we didn't stay there very long.
It was just a holding.
I think we didn't even do any work.
And then from there, we were sent to the Dachau.
It's a dream, like a dream.
I, I, I don't-- I don't think I remember that train.
It was just-- I know I went through it, but I don't know.
Can I be honest with you?
It's just like a dream.
The, the train ride was just like--
I remembered it was about three days.
We got in-- we got in the train in like, early in the morning.
The train didn't leave till at night.
It was cold.
It was freezing.
You didn't' have any food for the three days.
No food, no water, no nothing.
I think I must have passed out.
Yeah.
Just like herring.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I mean, you were-- you were laying on top of each other.
I don't know.
They must have had something in a corner or something.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Yeah.
That stayed with you all the time.
I'm sure they had it-- they had it registered over there when they give it to you.
I'm quite sure they did because they had-- instead of a name, they had your-- the number was you, was it, you know?
It was just a holding.
It was just almost like a big-- like a-- like a airplane hangar or something like that.
And we stayed there for a few days, and that's when we were by-- got back on the train and sent to Dachau.
On the floor.
They must have given us something.
Yeah.
They gave us some food over there, some rations, something.
I don't remember exactly what, but they gave us some food.
Yeah, the--
A soup and a portion of bread.
And the bread was always fresh.
They made us fresh, stale.
You swallowed it.
You ate it.
Yeah.
They would have give you some, you know, small portion.
They gave you to just trying to keep you alive, but not to live.
And then we wound up in Dachau.
Also by train.
Also by train, you know, because it was quite a distance.
I think it took another few days to go to Dachau.
And then in Dachau, we-- again, we weren't in the main camp.
We was in an outskirt.
And that camp was just being finished.
It wasn't even finished.
And it was like-- [CLEARS THROAT] Basically, what it was, it was-- it was in the woods.
There were-- we had to still clear the trees and stumps and everything.
And the way the barracks were was on the ground, like you take, take the ground.
Dig a ditch in the middle, you know, so like, you have-- and the ground was the bed on either side.
And it was like a triangle.
The roof was a triangle with like, wood or-- whatever it was.
And they had a stove right in the middle, just one little stove to keep-- it was supposed to keep you warm.
And they had about 50, or 60 people in that-- in these little barracks.
And I think we must have a blanket or something to cover.
But again, it wasn't-- it was already in November.
It was--
And it was cold.
It was snowing.
It was freezing.
And you didn't have any warm clothes.
And the people were freezing to dead.
And I was, you know, like-- and one of the guys from the three of us wound up in the kitchen, like doing a-- like in a mess hall, you know?
Like being a cook or something like that, so we had some extra food, you know, to, to live.
And, and then we went to work over there.
And the work we did over there was almost like, like, coal mining.
You know, we had this little-- on these little cars, railroad cars, the small cars.
I don't remember what they did, but we used to dig the-- put some dirt on.
Pushed the cars to maybe a half-- maybe half a mile or something like that.
Empty it, go back, and things like that.
And I remember in-- it was in January, the first part of January, I was uncoupling one of these cars.
And for some reason, my finger got caught.
And I-- you could still-- you see it?
And my finger-- tore off my finger, the whole thing.
And some of the skin.
It was red.
Things like that.
And this, one of the German engineers, whoever, saw it.
So he took me into their dispensary and he put it a band-aid on, a gauze.
It was no thing, just for stopping the bleeding.
And I had that band-aid on for about, I don't know, three days, four days, or five days.
And that gauze just kind of dried and just kind of grew, you know?
Like, became-- the blood was-- and things like that.
And I was trying to take it off, you know, myself.
And it was very painful to take it off.
So I tried to soak it in cold water.
And it was still-- I was doing it little bit at that time, trying to take it off.
And there was this one man there.
He was also a prisoner.
And he says to me, Morrie, let me take a-- let me look at it.
And I showed it to him.
And he just ripped it off, which was better, you know?
[LAUGHS] He just ripped it off all at once.
And then, I don't remember exactly how, but when he ripped it off, that, the German or something, he saw it.
And he took me in again to their dispensary.
And he a put a little cream on and re-bandaged it.
And it healed a little bit.
He was a German.
He was a-- he wasn't an SS man.
He was a civilian.
He was doing-- he was in charge-- he was an engineer in charge of the work.
And he was a human being.
I mean, he didn't like what, what was going on.
But he was a human being.
Oh, I don't think so.
I, I, I-- he wasn't afraid to do this.
I mean, it wasn't dangerous for him.
I mean, the SS weren't-- the SS weren't in charge of him.
He was a civilian engineer and things like that.
If they would've caught him, I don't think they would do anything to him.
They could have said, don't do this.
But he wasn't-- it wasn't any danger to him.
This camp that you mentioned, Dachau, but a little bit away from Dachau.
Yeah.
It did.
It did, but I don't remember.
I tell you another thing that I remember.
